quiz.title: Unit 3 Quiz 2, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Periodic Table Groups, Periods, and Classification
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Main-group Elements, Periods and Groups, Ion Formation, Noble Gases, Halogens, Lanthanides and Actinides, Mendeleev, Periodic Law

questions.question: Which of the following is a main-group element?
questions.explanation: Main-group elements are in Groups 1, 2, and 13–18. Oxygen (Group 16) is a main-group element. Iron, copper, and nickel are transition metals (d-block).

questions.question: Elements in the same period have the same number of:
questions.explanation: Elements in the same period (horizontal row) have the same number of occupied electron shells. For example, all elements in Period 2 have two electron shells.

questions.question: Which group contains elements that commonly form –1 ions?
questions.explanation: Group 17 elements (halogens) have seven valence electrons and tend to gain one electron to form –1 ions (e.g., Cl⁻, F⁻).

questions.question: Which group contains elements with full valence shells?
questions.explanation: Group 18 elements (noble gases) have full valence electron shells (octet, except helium with full 1s shell), making them chemically unreactive.

questions.question: The lanthanides and actinides are shown separately to:
questions.explanation: The lanthanides and actinides (f-block) are placed below the main periodic table to keep the table from becoming too wide and to improve readability.

questions.question: Which statement about groups is correct?
options: Groups have the same atomic mass
Groups have the same number of shells
questions.explanation: Groups (vertical columns) contain elements with the same number of valence electrons, resulting in similar chemical properties.

questions.question: Which statement about periods is correct?
options: Periods have the same valence electrons
questions.explanation: Periods are horizontal rows. Elements in the same period have the same number of electron shells but different valence electron counts.

questions.question: Which classification best describes elements like silicon and boron?
questions.explanation: Silicon and boron are metalloids—elements with properties intermediate between metals and nonmetals. They are found along the zigzag line on the periodic table.

questions.question: Who is credited with creating the first widely accepted periodic table?
questions.explanation: Dmitri Mendeleev (1869) created the first widely accepted periodic table. He arranged elements by atomic mass and left gaps for undiscovered elements, predicting their properties.

questions.question: Which quantum number primarily determines the period (row) in which an element is placed?
questions.explanation: The principal quantum number (n) determines the energy level and size of an electron orbital. The period number corresponds to the highest principal quantum number (n) of electrons in the atom's ground state configuration.

questions.question: The period 4 transition series begins with:
questions.explanation: The period 4 transition series begins with scandium (Sc, Z=21). Transition metals are defined by the filling of the d subshell. In period 4, the 3d subshell begins filling with scandium (electron configuration [Ar] 4s² 3d¹). Potassium (Z=19) and calcium (Z=20) are s-block elements, not transition metals.

questions.question: Which pair of elements would exhibit the most similar chemical properties?
questions.explanation: Fluorine (F) and chlorine (Cl) are both in Group 17 (halogens) and therefore have the same number of valence electrons (7), resulting in very similar chemical properties. Na and Mg are adjacent in Period 3, Fe and Cu are both transition metals but in different groups, and C and Pb are in different periods and groups with different valence electron configurations.

questions.question: The f-block elements are placed separately at the bottom of the periodic table to:
options: Show they have no chemical applications
questions.explanation: The f-block elements (lanthanides and actinides) are placed separately below the main periodic table primarily to prevent excessive horizontal expansion. Including them in their proper sequence would make the table extremely wide and impractical. This placement improves readability while maintaining the table's structure.

questions.question: Select all that apply: The periodic law states that properties of elements are periodic functions of their:
questions.explanation: The modern periodic law states that properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic number (B) and their electron configuration (C). Mendeleev's original law used atomic mass, but Moseley's work led to the atomic number-based law we use today.

questions.question: The lanthanide series consists of elements with atomic numbers:
questions.explanation: The lanthanide series includes elements with atomic numbers 57 (lanthanum) through 71 (lutetium). The actinide series is 89–103.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered periodic table groups, periods, and classification! 🧪📊
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the differences between main-group elements and transition metals.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on group properties and the location of different element types.
scoreMessages.0: Please review periodic table organization, including groups, periods, blocks, and element classifications.
